coredata.dc:description: Fe-doped carbon dots (FeCDs) with high peroxidase (POD)-like activity were prepared by a rapid microwave-assisted method and immobilized on cellulose nanofibrils (CNF) to produce a composite nanopaper. The POD activity of the nanopaper was evaluated by the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and applied to the colorimetric detections of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and glucose. CNF acted as a support for the nanozyme, thus imparting it with reusability. It also adsorbed the chromogen during the reaction to induce a color change, thus acting as a test strip for portable on-site detection. A smartphone was employed to monitor this color change which made the whole detection process simple and economical. Under optimal conditions, this method afforded linear ranges of 6–42 μM and 10–70 μM and detection limits of 0.93 and 1.73 μM for the H2O2 and glucose, respectively. The results of the quantification of glucose in human serum samples were comparable to those of the standard glucose oxidase (GOD)–POD method. Further, the nanopaper was potentially reused for ten cycles and presented over a month of shelf life.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of FeCDs (inset: HRTEM showing one particle), (b) the corresponding particle-size distribution histogram, and the (c) TEM image of CNF.
XRD patterns of (a) FeCDs and (b) CNF and CNF/FeCDs, and (c) FT–IR spectra of FeCDs, CNF, and CNF/FeCDs.
XPS analysis of the FeCDs: (a) survey spectrum, High-resolution scans of (b) Fe 2p, (c) C 1s, and (d) O 1s. XPS analysis of CNF and CNF/FeCDs: (e) comparative survey and (f) comparative C 1s spectra.

(a) Absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of FeCDs (inset: FeCDs aqueous solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right)). (b) Emission spectra of FeCDs at various excitation wavelengths. Digital photographs of CNF and CNF/FeCDs nanopapers under (c) day light and (d) 365 nm UV light.

(a) POD activity test for FeCDs (b) POD activity of FeCDs with and without a hydroxyl radical scavenger (IPA). Steady-state kinetic analysis employing the Michaelis–Menten and Lineweaver–Burk models (insets) for FeCDs with (c) TMB and (d) H2O2 as substrates. Error bars represent the standard error derived from three repeated measurements.

(a) Test for the POD activity of the CNF (top panel) and CNF/FeCDs (bottom panel) nanopapers: (i) control (ii) H2O2 (iii) TMB and (iv) TMB+H2O2 (b) Variation of the POD activity of the CNF/FeCDs nanopaper as a function of the FeCDs content. Error bars represent the standard error derived from three repeated measurements.

Linear responses of the color of the CNF/FeCDs nanopaper to the concentrations of (a) H2O2 and (b) glucose Inset: corresponding photographic images. Error bars represent the standard error derived from three repeated measurements.

Selectivity for glucose detection: (a) response to different substances and (b) the effect of their coexistence with glucose. (c) Stability and (d) recyclability of the nanopaper. Error bars represent the standard error derived from three repeated measurements.
Comparison of the H2O2- and glucose-detection performance of the CNF/FeCDs nanopaper with those of other peroxide-like systems.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fe-doped carbon dots (FeCDs) with high peroxidase (POD)-like activity were prepared by a rapid microwave-assisted method and immobilized on cellulose nanofibrils (CNF) to produce a composite nanopaper. The POD activity of the nanopaper was evaluated by the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and applied to the colorimetric detections of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and glucose. CNF acted as a support for the nanozyme, thus imparting it with reusability. It also adsorbed the chromogen during the reaction to induce a color change, thus acting as a test strip for portable on-site detection. A smartphone was employed to monitor this color change which made the whole detection process simple and economical. Under optimal conditions, this method afforded linear ranges of 6–42 μM and 10–70 μM and detection limits of 0.93 and 1.73 μM for the H2O2 and glucose, respectively. The results of the quantification of glucose in human serum samples were comparable to those of the standard glucose oxidase (GOD)–POD method. Further, the nanopaper was potentially reused for ten cycles and presented over a month of shelf life.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural enzymes are widely employed in the fields of agriculture, food processing, medicine, biochemical industry, and environmental science because of their high catalytic activity and substrate specificity [1]. However, their intrinsic downsides, such as poor stability, susceptibility to environmental changes, short storage life, and stringent working conditions, impinge on their practical application [2]. Moreover, the extraction and purification of an enzyme is a tedious, time-consuming, and expensive procedure [3]. Hence, the development of a highly stable and cost-effective enzyme mimic has become a hot research topic with increasing interest. Since the discovery of the peroxidase (POD)-mimicking activity of Fe3O4 by Yan’s group [4], numerous efforts have been invested to explore nanomaterials with enzyme-like activities (nanozymes). A vast range of nanomaterials, such as noble metals, metal oxide nanoparticles, carbon nanomaterials, bimetallic nanostructures, and metal–organic frameworks, have been confirmed as enzyme mimics [1,5]. Among them, carbon dots (CDs), a new member of the carbon-family nanomaterials, has attracted enormous attraction [6].

Since their serendipitous discovery, CDs have gained substantial attention with their remarkable features, such as their economic preparation process and functionalization, high stability, biocompatibility, water-solubility, tunable optical properties, and chemical inertness [7,8]. With these alluring properties, they have been successfully applied to the fields of bioimaging, cancer therapy, optoelectronics, sensing, photocatalysis, and forensic [9–13]. Moreover, the report on the POD activity of CDs by Shi et al.[14] has also exposed a new arena of applications. Compared to the existing nanozymes, CDs do not entail harsh synthetic conditions and expensive precursors. However, CDs still suffer from relatively low catalytic activity. Heteroatom doping, especially metal doping, is known to tune the intrinsic electronic characters of CDs and avail more active sites [15]. Additionally, the synergism between the metal atom and carbon in the lattice will improve the enzyme-mimic activity [16]. Thus, the preparation of metal-doped CDs is a promising strategy to enhance their catalytic activity. The POD activities of CDs doped with Pd [17], Pt [15], Cu [18], Co [19], and Fe [20] were studied recently, and among them, the doping with Fe is considered to be promising because of its inexpensiveness and Fenton-like ability.

The most common drawback of CDs and several other colloidal nanoparticle-based nanozymes is due to their poor recoverability and reusability, which are essential attributes of a catalyst. Immobilization on polymeric support is the most widely accepted solution to this challenge. Regarding sustainability, the utilization of a natural polymer is preferable to that of synthetic ones. Cellulose is the most abundant biopolymer in nature [21]. As a sustainable nanomaterial, nanocellulose can function best as a support for the immobilization of CDs because of its renowned properties, such as a large surface area, interesting mechanical properties, low density, biodegradability, and abundant surface functionalities [22,23]. Recently some authors reported the preparations of aerogels [24], hydrogels [25], and a nanopaper [26] by combining CDs with a nanocellulose matrix, followed by an exploration of their applications, and among them, papermaking by immobilizing CDs on cellulose nanofibrils (CNF) was advantageous for exploring their applications in colorimetric detection [27]. However, all the previous reports were based on the fluorescence behavior of CDs, and to the best of our knowledge, there are no available reports on the immobilization of POD-like CDs on nanocellulose.

In analytical chemistry, the developments of inexpensive, easy-to-utilize, portable, and fast response devices for rapid on-site detections are greatly desired [28]. In this expeditiously advancing age of technology, there is a significantly increased utilization of smartphones among people. With their high-resolution cameras and powerful computing capabilities, smartphones can measure the changes in color and light with high sensitivity [29]. Based on their digital image colorimetry, smartphones are evolving as competent analytical devices with applications in colorimetric and luminescence detections [30]. Hence, the combination of a nanopaper-based colorimetric probe with smartphones will significantly improve their applicability and facilitate the development of economical, handy, and user-friendly analytical devices.

In this work, Fe-doped CDs (FeCDs) with high POD-like activity were prepared by a rapid microwave-assisted method and immobilized on CNF to produce a composite nanopaper. The POD-like activity of the nanopaper was explored via the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and applied to the colorimetric detections of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and glucose. Further, to simplify this method, a smartphone was employed to monitor the color change in the nanopaper.
Refer supporting information for complete experimental details (materials, preparation of CNF, characterization and kinetic study).

FeCDs were synthesized utilizing citric acid (CA) as the carbon source and FeCl2.4H2O as the dopant. In the typical experiment, CA and FeCl2.4H2O were mixed in a mole ratio of 1:0.1 and ground to a fine powder. Thereafter, the powder was collected in a glass vial and subjected to microwave irradiation (MWI) employing a domestic MW oven operating at 700 W. The powder started melting after 6 min, and complete carbonization was achieved after 8 min of MWI. Afterward, 40 mL of deionized water (DW) was added and sonicated to achieve a clear dispersion. After adjusting the pH to neutral (with 50 % NaOH), it was filtered through 0.2 μ syringe filters. Next, dialysis was performed (MWCO 3.5 kDa) against DW for 3 days, and the product was concentrated by a rotary evaporator and lyophilized.

To a 2 g portion of CNF (1.5 wt%), 4 g of FeCDs aqueous dispersions of different concentrations were added to obtain solutions whose final CNF and FeCDs concentrations were 0.5 % and 1% (regarding the weight of CNF), respectively. The dispersion was stirred overnight to facilitate the interaction between FeCDs and CNF. Thereafter, the nanopaper was prepared by vacuum filtration and drying at the pump. Further, it was washed with 50 mL of DW during the filtration to remove the unbound FeCDs. Membrane filters (47 mm diameter and 0.2 μ pore size) were utilized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay for the detection of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: First, 100 μL of 1 mM TMB and 100 μL of H2O2 of various concentrations were added to 800 μL of the acetate buffer (pH 3.6). A CNF/FeCDs nanopaper strip was added to this solution and incubated at 25 °C for 20 min. Thereafter, the strip was transferred into a vial cap containing 100 μL of DW. The color intensity was recorded by keeping the strip under a custom-made smartphone colorimetric box. The strip without the addition of H2O2 was utilized as the blank.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay for the detection of glucose
ce:section.ce:para: The assay for the detection of glucose as follows [31]: (i) 10 μL of 5.0 mg/mL GOx and 20 μL of glucose of various concentrations were added to 70 μL of a phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4) and incubated at 37 °C for 15 min to produce H2O2; (ii) 100 μL of 1 mM TMB and 800 μL of an acetate buffer (pH 3.6) were mixed with the previous glucose and GOx reaction mixture; (iii) after adding the CNF/FeCDs nanopaper strip, the above mixture solution was incubated for 20 min at 25 °C. Subsequently, the strip was transferred into a vial cap containing 100 μL of DW and the color intensity was recorded as aforementioned.

A smartphone-based colorimetric assay was developed for the detections of H2O2 and glucose. A “Realme 3 pro” mobile with dual camera (16 M P + 5 M P) was utilized. The color intensity of the nanopaper was recorded by “Color Grab,” which is a free android app. A custom-made box (Fig.S1) was employed to ensure a dark environment; the flashlight of the smartphone served as the light source and the distance between the camera and sample was fixed. All these settings would avoid the influence of external lighting conditions on the measurements. In principle, the color intensity of the nanopaper was directly proportional to the concentration of H2O2. However, the color intensity of a digital image could be measured by different color space parameters, such as RGB, CMYK, grayscale, and Lab. Therefore, it was essential to determine the most suitable relationship for the quantification of a scanned image. For this purpose another free android app “PhotoMetrix PRO” was used. This app in the calibration mode, afforded the linear equations of eight commonly utilized color channels, i.e., R, G, B, H, S, L, V, and I. Among these, channel R afforded the best linear correlation (Fig.S2). To minimize the experimental errors, R0–R (R0 and R are the intensities of the R channels of the control and experimental samples, respectively) was adopted in this study.

The serum samples were acquired from the Biobank of Chungnam National University Hospital, a member of the Korean Biobank Network. The obtained samples were centrifuged to eliminate the possible interference of the protein deposits. The supernatant was properly diluted with PBS (0.1 M and pH 7). The assay for the detection of glucose was performed, as mentioned in Section 2.4. For comparison, the same samples were analyzed by the standard GOD–POD method [32]. The study was approved by the ethics committees of the institutions involved.

A novel solid-state microwave-assisted carbonization method was developed for the production of FeCDs. Unlike the previously reported time-consuming high-temperature hydrothermal reactions, this method was rapid and green. A domestic microwave oven operating with a power of 700 W was utilized, and it required 8 min for carbonization. The mole ratio of the reactants was optimized as CA:FeCl2.4H2O = 1:0.1 by considering both the fluorescence emission and POD activity of the as-obtained FeCDs (Fig. S3). Interestingly, CA alone could not produce significant emission (CDs) under the same conditions, implying that the presence of the metal salt favored CDs formation. This was also observed in the production of Pt-doped CDs [15].

CNF was prepared from softwood pulp via the TEMPO-oxidation, followed by ultrasonication. During the reaction, the regioselective oxidation of the primary OH groups at the 6th position to the carboxylate groups occurred, thus manifesting an electrostatic repulsion between the adjacent nanofibrils. This process aided the separation of the nanofibrils from the bundles and stabilized the obtained CNF suspension. The carboxylate content of CNF estimated by conductometric titration (Fig. S4) was 0.81 mmol/g and this value agrees with those of previous reports on TEMPO oxidation of CNF [33]. The CNF/FeCDs composite nanopaper was prepared by an overnight treatment of the mixture of CNF and FeCDs, followed by filtration and drying at the vacuum pump.

The TEM observations were employed to explore the morphological features of FeCDs and CNF. The TEM image of FeCDs at a magnification of 20 nm (Fig. 1 a) disclosed the spherical morphology and well-dispersed particles. No clear lattice fringes were observed in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1 a inset), indicating the amorphous nature of FeCDs. The corresponding size-distribution analysis (Fig. 1b) unveiled their narrow size distribution in the range of 2–7 nm with a mean diameter of 4.0 ± 0.9 nm, which confirmed that it was a good candidate for doping additive. The TEM observation of CNF (Fig. 1c) revealed a diameter of 4–8 nm and a length of several microns. Further, well-dispersed nanofibrils with an entangled structure, high aspect ratio, and a nanoscale diameter were observed. All these properties make CNF a good candidate for the trapping of FeCDs and the preparation of a transparent nanopaper [34].

The powder XRD pattern of FeCDs (Fig. 2 a) exhibited a halo in the 2θ range of 23°–35° centered at 29°, indicating its poor crystallization. This result follows that of HRTEM [35]. The effect of the incorporation of FeCDs on the crystal structure of CNF was evaluated by comparing the XRD patterns of CNF and CNF/FeCDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, the two major peaks at 15.3° and 22.5° that corresponded to the characteristic (110) and (200) reflections of the cellulose I crystal structure were observed in both samples. Furthermore, the crystallinity index (CrI) calculated by the Segal equation did not also reveal significant changes (69.42 % for CNF and 69.48 % for CNF/FeCDs). These results confirmed that FeCDs were uniformly distributed throughout the CNF matrix without disrupting its crystal structure. Similar results were also observed in previous studies [36].

The FT–IR spectra of FeCDs, CNF, and CNF/FeCDs are shown in Fig. 2 c. In the FT–IR spectrum of FeCDs, the peaks at 3436 and 1397 cm−1were assigned to the stretching and in-plane bending vibrations of OH, respectively [37]. The peaks in the range of 1800–1480 cm−1 were ascribable to the CO and CC functional groups and the peak at 1258 could be assigned to the sp2 C − O stretching [38]. The results confirm the presence of polar carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups (on the surface of FeCDs) that could interact with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of CNF via hydrogen bonding. In the FT–IR spectrum of CNF, the broad absorption band at 3330 cm−1 and the small peak at 2900 cm−1correspond to the stretching vibrations of the OH and CH groups, respectively. The absorption peak at 1604 cm−1, which corresponded to sodium carboxylate, confirmed the oxidation process of TEMPO. The peak at 1315 cm−1 correlates with CH2 wagging, and the peaks at 1160 and 1026 cm−1 were assigned to the COC asymmetric stretch and valence vibrations, respectively. The peak at 895 cm−1 was assigned to the CH deformation vibrations of cellulose. Moreover, the absence of a peak at 1026 cm−1 for FeCDs indicated that this peak was suitable for comparison. Regarding the peak, the relative intensities of OH, CH, −COO, and −CH2 were calculated for both the CNF and CNF/FeCDs samples. As shown in Table S1, after the immobilization of FeCDs, the relative intensities of the OH, CH, −COO, and −CH2 groups of CNF increased by 21.75 %, 19.51 %, 7.78 %, and 9.95 %, respectively. For a specific functional group, the intensity of the IR absorption peak is relative to hydrogen bonds, conjugate groups, fermi resonance, and solvent effects [39]. Moreover, the concentration also influences the vibrational band intensity. However, the influence could be neglected since the intensity ratio was adopted for comparison. Since both samples (CNF and CNF/FeCDs) exhibited the same vibrational peaks, the effects of the conjugate groups and Fermi resonance could be omitted. Further, in the preparations of both samples, water was utilized as the solvent, thereby ruling out the solvent effect. Conclusively, the increase in the vibrational peak intensities was attributed to the hydrogen-bond formation. This implied that FeCDs were trapped in the entangled network of CNF and were held by hydrogen bonding [40].

XPS was employed to analyze the chemical composition and surface states of the samples. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3 a) of FeCDs presented three peaks at around 285, 532, and 710 eV corresponding to the C 1s, O 1s, and Fe 3p orbitals, respectively. The appearance of the Fe2p peaks confirmed the successful doping of Fe in the carbon lattice. Additionally, the elemental composition revealed the doping content of Fe to be 2.75 %. Further, Fe 2p could be resolved into four peaks (Fig. 3b) corresponding to the four Fe species, namely Fe2+ 2p3/2 (709.6 eV), Fe3+ 2p3/2 (713.2 eV), Fe2+ 2p1/2 (723.6 eV), and Fe3+ 2p1/2 (726.7 eV). The appearance of the Fe3+ peaks indicated the partial oxidation of Fe2+ during the synthesis. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) could be resolved into three peaks corresponding to the 3 types of carbon bonds, namely CC/C = C at 285.0 eV, CO at 287.3 eV, and CO at 288.8 eV. As shown in Fig. 3d, O 1s could be resolved into two peaks at 531.4 eV and 533.3 eV corresponding to the CO and CO groups, respectively.

As depicted in Fig. 3e, the XPS survey spectra of CNF and CNF/FeCDs revealed the presence of C, O, and traces of Na. The peak corresponding to Fe 3p was invisible in CNF/FeCDs probably because of the low loading content. The elemental compositions obtained from XPS analysis are also shown in Fig. 3 e. The obtained O/C ratios of CNF and FeCDs were 0.60 and 0.65, respectively (Table S2). However, this value decreased to 0.50 for the composite nanopaper, indicating the binding interaction [41]. Moreover, the peak-shifting in the high-resolution C 1s scan (Fig. 3f) further confirmed the binding interaction.

UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopic studies were conducted to investigate the optical properties of FeCDs. The absorption spectrum of FeCDs (Fig. 4 a) disclosed an apparent absorption in the UV region with a tail that extended to the visible region. There was no specific absorption peak. The fluorescence spectra of FeCDs exhibited an optimum excitation (370 nm) and a corresponding emission at 480 nm with a large Stokes shift of 110 nm that was ascribed to the introduction of the heteroatom (Fe). The digital photographs of the FeCDs aqueous dispersion (Fig. 4 a inset) captured in daylight disclosed a translucent, pale-yellow colored solution with a bright blue emission under UV light. The fluorescence property of FeCDs was further investigated by recording the emission spectra at various excitations. As shown in Fig. 4 b & S5, a clear redshift in λem (from 445 to 483 nm) was observed as λex increased from 300 to 400 nm. This type of excitation-dependent emission behavior might be due to the presence of various surface energy traps (SETs) on CDs [42]. The fluorescence quantum yield calculated with QS as a reference compound was 9.3 %. Digital photographs of CNF and CNF/FeCDs nanopaper under day light and UV light are shown in Fig. 4 c & d. Under daylight, CNF appeared as white and transparent, whereas CNF/FeCDs was pale yellow and transparent. Under UV light no emission was observed from CNF whereas CNF/FeCDs nanopaper exhibited bright blue emission. This observation makes it clear that the FeCDs were uniformly distributed throughout the nanopaper and the nanofibrils prevent the FeCDs from aggregation. This kind of solid state emission is quite useful in fluorescent displays and other applications [43].

The POD activity of FeCDs was evaluated by catalyzing the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to obtain blue-colored ox-TMB. The absorption spectra and corresponding digital images of the TMB, (TMB + H2O2), (TMB + FeCDs), and (TMB + H2O2 + FeCDs) systems in acetate buffer (pH 3.6) after 20 min of interaction are shown in Fig. 5 a. Therein, the (TMB + H2O2) mixed solution exhibited a typical blue color in the presence of FeCDs with an absorption maximum at 652 nm, which corresponded to the oxidation product of TMB. However, the same (TMB + H2O2) solution failed to exhibit this kind of coloration in the absence of FeCDs, thus confirming the POD activity of FeCDs. Based on the literature [44], the origin of this POD-like activity was ascribed to the Fe species in FeCDs that acted as Fenton’s reagent to catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into a reactive hydroxyl substance (·OH), which in turn oxidized TMB. To verify the role of ·OH in the oxidation of TMB, catalytic reactions were performed in the presence of Iso-propanol (IPA), a radical scavenger, that could rapidly react with a highly active ·OH radical to produce inert intermediate radicals [45]. Compared to the control, fewer amounts of TMB were oxidized in the presence of IPA, as indicated by the decreased absorbance of ox-TMB (Fig. 5b). This result confirmed that ·OH was the active oxygen species in the TMB oxidation system with FeCDs. To further investigate the POD-like behavior of FeCDs, steady-state kinetic experiments were performed. According to the reported procedure [46], a series of experiments were performed by varying the concentrations of TMB and H2O2. The Michaelis–Menten curves obtained in a certain range of the substrate concentrations are shown in Figs. 5c & d. The Lineweaver–Burk plots were applied to calculate the maximum initial velocity (Vm) and the Michaelis–Menten constant (Km). The Km value is an essential parameter for the determination of the binding affinity of an enzyme for the substrate, and a low Km value denotes a strong affinity and vice versa [15]. As shown in the insets of Figs. 5c & d, the obtained Km and Vmax values for TMB and H2O2 were 0.194 mM, 13.11 × 10−8Ms−1 and 0.385 mM, 12.58 × 10−8Ms−1, respectively. The Km value of FeCDs for TMB was lower than that of H2O2, further implying the high affinity of FeCDs for TMB. These results correlate well with those of previous reports, and it highlighted the capability of FeCDs for the colorimetric detection of H2O2 [47]. Interestingly, the Km values for TMB and H2O2 were lower than that of a natural enzyme, horseradish POD (HRP), indicating the high POD activity of the FeCDs nanozyme [20].

Similarly, the POD activity of the CNF/FeCDs composite nanopaper was studied. The nanopaper (diameter, 35 mm) was cut into small circles (diameter, 6 mm each) with an office punch and utilized as the test strips. As shown in Fig. S6, 12 strips could be produced from one nanopaper. Firstly, the POD activity was confirmed by adding the CNF and CNF/FeCDs strips to the buffer solutions containing H2O2, TMB and (TMB + H2O2). Fig. 6 a shows that only the CNF/Fe-CDs strip in the (TMB + H2O2) system was converted to blue color, indicating its POD activity and successful immobilization of FeCDs. Furthermore, Fe2+ and Fe3+ incorporated CNF nanopapers were prepared and their peroxidase activity was evaluated by similar procedures. As shown in Fig.S7, these nanopapers were also converted to blue. This result further supported our assumption of doped iron species are indeed responsible for the peroxidase activity. The color change of the nanopaper instead of the solution was ascribed to the adsorption of positively charged amine-containing ox-TMB on the negatively charged carboxylate containing surface of CNF.

To further examine the POD-like activity of the CNF/FeCD nanopaper, the influences of various parameters, such as pH, temperature, the concentration of the substrate (TMB), and incubation time, on the color intensity of the nanopaper were studied. The variation in the color intensity (R0–R) of the nanopaper was recorded with Color Grab app (Fig.S8). The amount of catalyst employed was first studied. For this purpose, nanopapers with different FeCDs amounts (0.5–2 wt%) regarding CNF were prepared. As shown in Fig. 6b, the highest color response was observed at 1%. The observed decreased response at high FeCDs dose did not indicate a decreased catalytic activity; it was due to the initial color of the nanopaper. In the POD reaction, pH was crucial and an acidic pH was observed to be favorable [46]. Hence, the effects of various pH values from 2 to 7 were studied (Fig. S9a). As the pH increased, a clear decrease was observed in the activity, and maximum color intensity was observed at pH 3.6. The effect of temperature on the catalytic activity is studied to evaluate the temperature tolerance of this artificial enzyme. The good activity was observed over a temperature range of 20–40 °C, and the highest response was observed at 25 °C, which correlated with the optimum temperature for the assay (Fig. S9b). As shown in Fig. S9c, the color intensity increased as the reaction time increased. It attained a maximum value after 20 min, and this time was selected as the optimum. Moreover, an adverse effect was observed on the color of the nanopaper after prolonged interaction (> 50 min). This might be due to the protonation of CNF that weakened its interaction with TMB and caused leaching. This implied there was a time restriction for this assay. Furthermore, the blue color of the nanopaper was susceptible to fading due to drying for a prolonged time. Fortunately, these limitations were overcome by transferring the nanopaper into DW. Therein, the nanopaper retained its blue color for more than one day. The concentration of TMB also exhibited a significant influence on the coloration of the nanopaper. As depicted in Fig. S9d, high color intensity was achieved with 0.1 mM TMB. Overall the optimal conditions required for the high POD activity are pH 3.6 at a temperature of 25 °C employing 0.1 mM TMB for 20 min of incubation.

Recently, the utilization of H2O2 for water purification via advanced oxidation processes, for sterilization in the food industry, and biochemical procedures have been high [48]. Hence, the quantifications of H2O2 in environmental and biological samples are of vital significance. Since the oxidation of TMB and the blue coloration of the nanopaper were dependent on the concentration of H2O2, the system was applied to detect H2O2. Under the optimal conditions, the color intensity of the nanopaper was recorded as a function of the concentration of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 7 a, a linear response was observed in the concentration range of 6–42 μM. The linear regression to fit the data was R0–R = 18.615 + 3.57113 [H2O2]. The Limit of detection (LOD) obtained from the equation, 3σ/m (where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and m the slope of the linear fit), was 0.93 μM.

Currently, there is an increased interest in the estimation of the amount of glucose present in a buffer or real sample because of the annual growth in the number of diabetic patients[49]. H2O2 is the main product of glucose oxidation by GOx, and the proposed probe could monitor H2O2. Therefore, the nanopaper was expected to detect glucose in the presence of GOx. This was verified by recording the color responses of the nanopaper at various concentrations of glucose in the co-presence of GOx. As shown in Fig. 7b, a linear response was observed in the range of 10–70 μM, and the linear equation to fit the data was R0–R = −1.619 + 1.90405 [Glucose], and LOD was 1.73 μM. The analytical features of the proposed method are in good agreement with those of previously reported methods on nanozyme-based glucose detection (Table 1 ).

The selective response to the target analyte is a prerequisite feature of any probe. To evaluate this, control experiments were performed with fructose, maltose, lactose, and sucrose. As shown in Fig. 8 a, no evident change was observed in the color response of the nanopaper even at five-time higher concentrations than glucose, thus signifying the high selectivity of the probe. The interference of these species was also studied by the addition of glucose to the same samples. In the co-presence of glucose and other species, it was observed that the nanopaper exhibited a similar response as that with glucose alone (Fig. 8b). Furthermore, no significant interference was observed in the individual presence ten biologically relevant metal ions (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, and Mg2+) each at a concentration five times higher than glucose (Fig. S10). All these results represent the great selectivity of the probe.

The probe was employed to detect glucose in human serum samples to evaluate its practical applicability. Before the analysis, the serum samples, which were obtained from apparently healthy volunteers, were properly diluted to ensure that the glucose contents of the samples were in the detection range of the proposed method. For comparison, the standard GOD–POD method was also employed, and the analytical results of both methods are presented in Table 2 . The analysis results of the three human serum samples obtained by the proposed method were closely matched with those of the standard GOD–POD method, indicating the potential of the proposed method for the quantification of glucose in clinically relevant samples.

The shelf life of the nanopaper-based probe was assessed by recording the color response of the nanopaper stored under ambient conditions at different time intervals for over 30 days. As shown in Fig. 8c, the nanopaper retained 90 % of its initial response even after 30 days. This high stability was advantageous and was attributable to the uniform dispersion in the matrix of the nanocellulose, which prevented the aggregation of FeCDs. To date, several POD-mimicking nanozymes have been reported but their recyclabilities were less explored because of the tedious steps involved in recovering colloidal nanozymes [1]. Particularly, CDs with their small sizes and high hydrophilic nature was almost impossible to recover. In this work, as the FeCDs were immobilized on nanocellulose support they were easily recovered after usage. The complete disappearance of the blue color was observed after one minute treatment with 0.1 M ascorbic acid that reduces the ox-TMB. Thereafter, the nanopaper was washed with DW, dried and reused. Interestingly, the nanopaper retained its activity for up to ten cycles ( Fig. 8d) without a significant loss in activity, signifying its potential for multiple usages.

A rapid microwave-assisted solid-state carbonization method was developed to synthesize FeCDs with high POD activity. FeCDs were immobilized on CNF that were obtained by the oxidation of TEMPO. FeCDs were uniformly dispersed throughout the CNF matrix, strongly held by hydrogen-bonding interactions, and did not disrupt the structure and properties of the cellulose. The immobilization of FeCDs on CNF prevented their aggregation, thus retaining their POD activity. Regarding the POD activity, the CNF/FeCDs composite nanopaper was employed for the colorimetric detections of H2O2 and glucose employing TMB as the chromogen. The color change in the nanopaper was monitored by a smartphone, which rendered the detection process easy and inexpensive. CNF served as an excellent matrix for the nanozyme, which imparted it with reusability and stability for long term storage. Furthermore, the probe exhibited promising results for glucose detection in human serum samples. As a whole, the probe with the advantages of high sensitivity, selectivity, easy handling, and recyclability has great potential for applications in biomedicine, biotechnology, and environmental chemistry.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rajkumar Bandi received his Ph.D. (2019) from Department of Chemistry, Osmania University, India. Currently he is a researcher at Institute of Forest Science, Kangwon National University. His current research interests include development of metal nanoparticles and carbon dots based optical sensors, and biomaterials for sustainability and catalysis.

Madhusudhan Alle is currently working as a Research Professor at Institute of Forest Science, Kangwon National University. He received his doctorate from Department of Chemistry, Osmania University, India. His current research interests include polymeric nano drug delivery systems, cancer biology, biomaterials for sustainability and catalysis.

Chan-Woo Park received his BSc (2015), MSc (2017), and Ph.D. (2020) from Department of Forest BioMaterials and Engineering, Kangwon National University, republic of Korea. Currently he is a researcher at Institute of Forest Science, Kangwon National University. His main research interests involve characteristics of nanocellulose, biomass pretreatment, Bio-nanocomposites, and bioplastic, and 3D-printing.

Song-Yi Han received her BSc (2013), MSc and Ph.D. (2020) from Department of Forest BioMaterial and Engineering, Kangwon National University, Republic of Korea. Currently she is a researcher at Institute of Forest Science, Kangwon National University. Her research interests involve biomass pretreatment, bioenergy and biofuel.

Gu-Joong Kwon received his BSc (1992), MSc (1998), and Ph.D. (2002) from Kangwon National University, Republic of Korea. He was nominated as a research professor at Kangwon Institute of Inclusive Technology, and Institute of Forest Science. His research interests focus on the characterization of cellulose aerogels and nanocelluloses based on various porous structures.

Nam-Hun Kim received his BSc (1981), MSc (1986) from Department of Forest Product Processing, Kangwon National University, Republic of Korea. He obtained his Ph.D. (1990) from Tokyo University, Japan. In 1990, he was nominated as a professor in Kangwon National University, Republic of Korea. He was a president of Korean Society of Wood Science & Technology (2016). He is a full member of International Academy of Wood Science (IAWA) (2018). His research interests involve wood anatomy, physics of wood, wood carbonization, wood utilization.

Jin-Chul Kim is currently working as a Professor at Department of Medical Biomaterials Engineering, Kangwon National University, South Korea, since 2003. Earlier he worked as a senior researcher at LG Household & healthcare, Republic of Korea (1998–2003). He completed his Post-doctoral research at Department of Industrial and Physical Pharmacy (IPPH), Purdue University, USA (1997–1998). He received his Doctorate from Department of Chemical Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST). His current research interests include drug delivery systems, colloid & interfaces science and polymer science.

Seung-Hwan Lee received his BSc (1993) and MSc (1995) from Kangwon National University, Republic of Korea. He obtained his Ph.D. (2000) from the Postgraduate School, Kyoto University, Japan. He was a senior researcher in Green Bio Institute, Japan (2000–2004), a senior research scientist in National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST), Japan (2006–2012) and an adjunct assistant professor in University of Tennessee, US (2008–2013). In 2012, he was nominated as a professor in Kangwon National University, Republic of Korea. His research interests involve biomass, bioenergy, nanoscience for forest products, chemical modification of lignocellulose, novel bio-based polymers, and bio-nanocomposites.
